title: Topological defects in out-of-equilibrium systems
abstract: In this PhD thesis, we study topological defects in two-dimensional non-equilibrium systems, focusing on active extensions of the XY model, including activity, mobility and non-reciprocity. In a noisy Kuramoto lattice with short-range coupling, intrinsic frequency heterogeneity destroys quasi-long-range order and fragments the system into finite domains. Defects unbind at all temperatures and exhibit superdiffusive random walks, advected by evolving domain boundaries. By contrast, when oscillators are allowed to move in space, the system undergoes a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition and regains quasi-long-range order, revealing the fundamental role of motility in sustaining coherence. We also analyse a non-reciprocal O(2) model with vision-cone couplings and derive a continuum theory that captures the same large-scale physics. Non-reciprocity selects defect shapes, enriches the annihilation process, and reshapes patterns through advection. Together, these results elucidate the fundamental role of activity and non-reciprocity in shaping topological defects and ordering in non-equilibrium systems.   Keywords: Topological defects, XY model, Steep XY model, Kuramoto model, Non-reciprocal interactions, Active matter, Phase transitions, Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition, Non-equilibrium statistical mechanics
summary: This work explores how non-equilibrium features like activity and non-reciprocity alter the behavior of topological defects in 2D systems. It demonstrates that while frequency heterogeneity can destroy order, particle mobility can restore the BKT transition, and non-reciprocity introduces novel defect dynamics.
main_problem: Investigating how activity, mobility, and non-reciprocity influence topological defect dynamics and the stability of ordered phases in two-dimensional non-equilibrium systems.
main_result: Frequency heterogeneity in Kuramoto lattices destroys quasi-long-range order, but allowing oscillator mobility restores the Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition; additionally, non-reciprocity is shown to shape defects and drive pattern advection.
method: Numerical simulations using Langevin dynamics on square and triangular lattices, coupled with the derivation of a continuum field theory for non-reciprocal models.
model_or_system: Active extensions of the XY model, including a noisy Kuramoto lattice with short-range coupling, a mobile Kuramoto model, and a non-reciprocal O(2) model with vision-cone couplings.
key_observables: Topological defect density, defect charge, defect shape, spatial correlation functions, and defect-driven superdiffusive random walks.
important_parameters: Temperature, activity, mobility, non-reciprocity, intrinsic frequency heterogeneity, and the steepness parameter (p).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on 2D systems and explores the transition from lattice-based models to continuum field theories.
figures_summary: Sketches of topological defects in polar and nematic fields, examples of defects in 2D crystal lattices, and real-world biological/physical analogues like penguin huddles and fingerprints.
paper_structure: The thesis begins with an introduction to topological defects and active matter, followed by detailed studies of the Kuramoto model (heterogeneity vs. mobility), the non-reciprocal O(2) model, and the steep XY model, concluding with a continuum theory derivation.
why_interesting: The study of non-reciprocal interactions and the restoration of phase transitions through mobility provides deep insights into the emergence of order in driven-dissipative and active many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1.1: Sketches of different topological defects, of charge q, for polar order (a-c) and nematic (d,e) orientation fields. Image slightly adapted from [47]. (a) Vortex, charge q = +1. The direction field (arrows) rotates by +2π following a positive contour (in the counterclock- wise direction) around the defect core, in red. (b) Source, charge q = +1. The direction field also picks a +2π phase along a positive contour, but contrarily to the vortex configuration, the arrows point radially outwards from the core (no rotational, only divergence component). (c) Charge q = −1 defect: the arrows rotates by −2π following a positive contour around the defect core. (d) Comet shaped q = +1/2...

Figure 1.2: (a) Typical topological defects in a 2d crystal lattice of densely packed spherical particles. Image and caption from [48]. Particles in 5-fold cells are colored in red, 7-fold ones in blue, and all others in grey. Two disclinations are shown in (b), a dislocation and a disclination in (c), a vacancy in (d), and a detailed view of two clusters of defects belonging to the same grain boundary (shown in yellow in (a) and delimiting regions with different hexatic order) in (e).

Figure 1.3: Examples of topological defects in various systems: (a) Different states of matter in Emperor penguin populations (image from [49]) (b) A fish ball (image from [50]) (c, d) fingerprints, with topological defects highlighted in pink (image from [51]) (e) nematic liquid crystal in an LCD screen (cover from [52]) (f) Topological defects as organisation centers in Hydra [53].

Figure 2.1: (a) The correlation function C(r). The coloured dashed lines are the best fits ∼r−η. The black dash line is 0.71r−1/4. (b) The correlation length η, extracted from C(r) ∼r−η. The dashed line is T/(2π), the dotted horizontal line is η = 1/4, the vertical dotted line is T = TKT = 0.89. These measurements were performed for L = 128 because they are numerically more demanding (average over more realizations and longer equilibration times are needed), in particular close to the critical temperature, explaining the shift of the critical temperature value from the large L limit TKT ≈0.89.

Figure 2.2: Four +1 defects with different shapes (a) µ+ = 0 (b) µ+ = π/2 (c) µ+ = 3π/4 (d) µ+ = π. White dots show the defect core, on the dual (hexagonal) lattice. The colour code displays the phase θ of each spin.
